does: This paper operationalizes a thermodynamic framework for model safety by defining ethical entropy as a measurable state variable tracking value drift in large language models. The authors develop a five-way behavioral taxonomy and train a classifier to estimate ethical entropy S(t) from model transcripts across stress tests, comparing base and instruction-tuned variants of four frontier models. They demonstrate that base models exhibit sustained entropy growth while tuned variants suppress drift and reduce ethical entropy by approximately eighty percent. From these entropy trajectories, they estimate an effective alignment work rate γ_eff and implement a monitoring pipeline that flags alerts when entropy drift exceeds stability thresholds, providing operational run-time oversight of value degradation in deployed models.
connects: This work builds on recent theoretical frameworks treating intelligence through thermodynamic principles, specifically extending a Second Law of Intelligence that characterizes ethical alignment as work that counters natural entropy increase. It responds to limitations of static safety benchmarks by addressing dynamic failure modes including distribution shift, jailbreak vulnerability, and gradual degradation during deployment. The approach connects to broader AI safety literature on value drift and alignment verification, while drawing inspiration from thermodynamic monitoring in other safety-critical systems. The use of behavioral classifiers and transcript analysis follows established NLP methods for model auditing, extending them to quantify alignment degradation rather than single-point failures.
enables: This framework enables continuous monitoring of alignment drift in production systems, allowing practitioners to detect value degradation before catastrophic failures occur. The operational measure of alignment work rate γ_eff could support adaptive retraining strategies that maintain safety properties under distribution shift. The behavioral taxonomy and entropy measurement approach may generalize to other safety properties beyond ethics, potentially applying to truthfulness, robustness, and other alignment dimensions. Future work could integrate this monitoring pipeline into automated safeguards that trigger retraining or intervention when entropy thresholds are breached, or use entropy dynamics to optimize alignment interventions by identifying which techniques most effectively reduce drift under specific conditions.
